title: Two types of corticopulvinar terminations: Round (type 2) and elongate (type 1)
abstract:
THE JOURNAL OF COMPARATIVE NEUROLOGY 368~57-87 (1996) 
Two Types of 
Corticopulvinar Terminations: 
Round (Type 2) and Elongate (Type 1) 
KATHLEEN S. ROCKLAND 
Department of Neurology, Division of Behavioral Neurology and Cognitive Neuroscience, 
University of Iowa, College of Medicine, Iowa City, Iowa 52242 
ABSTRACT 
Corticopulvinar axons were anterogradely labeled by Phaseolus vulgaris-leucoagglutinin 
injections in the occipitotemporal cortex of the macaque to determine quantitative parameters 
of divergence and convergence, arbor size and shape, and distribution of terminal specializa- 
tions. Forty individual axons were analyzed by serial section reconstruction and divided into 
two major groups. The majority of axons have numerous (typically 500-1,000) small, spinous 
endings (boutons terminaux). These axons have terminal fields that are beam-like or elongated 
(E, corresponding to classical type 1) and highly divergent (1.0-3.0 mm). These frequently 
innervate several of the traditionally designated pulvinar subdivisions; namely inferior 
pulvinar (PI) and the ventral part of interal pulvinar (PL); medial pulvinar (PM) and dorsal PL, 
and (one axon) PM, dorsal PL, and PI. Some axons, however (R or round, corresponding to 
classical type 21, have a small number (typically 70-160) of primarily large, beaded endings 
(boutons en passant), which concentrate in sharply delimited, round arbors (diameters 100-125 
pm). R axons appear to be larger caliber than E axons (1.0-1.5 pm vs. 0.5-1.0 pm, respectively). 
These differences in phenotype are probably associated with distinct types of projection 
neurons. In visual areas, corticopulvinar terminations are reported to originate from pyramidal 
cell subpopulations in layer 5. Indirect evidence, presented here, suggests that the more 
numerous medium-sized neurons give rise to E axons, and the sparser giant pyramids give rise 
to R corticopulvinar axons. If this is correct, corticopulvinar connectivity may be involved in 
multiple transformations. Spatially, axons of giant neurons (with basal dendrites that collect 
intracortically from a disc-like area, about 1.0 mm in diameter) converge onto a small number of 
pulvinar neurons. Axons of medium neurons (with basal dendrites that occupy a small 
intracortical disc, about 0.3 mm in diameter) diverge over 1.0-3.0 mm in the pulvinar and may 
form many contacts. Giant neurons, although numerically few in relation to medium pyramids 
(1 or 2: 50?), are likely to have distinctive membrane properties (functionally equivalent to 
bursting neurons?). Their larger boutons and axon caliber may be associated with a faster 
transmission that compensates for their small numbers. In primates, the E and R duality does 
not characterize cortical projections to the caudate, lateral geniculate nucleus, pons, or super- 
ior colliculus and thus may be essentially linked to pulvinar-specific processes. 
R> 1996 Wiley-Liss, Inc. 
Indexing terms: axon terminal distribution, PHA-L, pulvinar, thalamus, visual system 
The involvement of the pulvinar complex1 in mechanisms 
of visual perception is well supported by a variety of 
evidence, but the specific roles and even functional architec- 
ture of this large nucleus remain relatively enigmatic (for 
recent reviews, see Kaas and Huerta, 1988; Desimone et al.,

'The pulvinar has traditionally been subdivided into four suhnuclei: the 
inferior (PI), lateral (PL), medial (PM), and oral (Jones, 1985). As frequently 
noted, these subdivisions do not correspond particularly well to either visual 
field maps or to neurochemical markers. Gutierrez et al. (19951, for example, 
propose a revised terminology in which PI is identified as a neurochemically 
distinct region that includes the traditional cytoarchitectonic nucleus PI and 
adjacent portions of PL and PM. In the present study, I have opted for the 
classical nomenclature, because this still seems to provide the broadest 
o 1996 WILEY-LISS, INC. 
 58 
connections from various structures-including 
the retina, 
superior colliculus, and amygdala-a 
conspicuous feature 
is the association with neocortex. This connectional system 
is dense, generally reciprocal, and presumably functionally 
important. Thus, visual responsivity in PI is affected by 
inactivation of striate cortex but not of the superior collicu- 
lus (Bender, 1983). In at least one cortical area, middle 
temporal (MI'), residual physiological activity, remaining 
after inactivation of V1, disappears after a subsequent 
lesion of the superior colliculus, presumably because this 
interrupts a tectopulvinocortical pathway to MT (Rodman 
et al., 1990; Bullier et al., 1994). 
Pulvinocortical connections, anterogradely labeled by 
"-amino 
acids or wheat germ agglutinin-horseradish per- 
oxidase (WGA-HRP), are known to terminate in layer 1 in 
area V1, and in several layers in other cortical areas. These 
are described as layers 1, 3, 4, and 5, with some vari- 
ability according to area, species, and possibly technique 
(Benevento and Rezak, 1976; Trojanowski and Jacobson, 
1976; Ogren and Hendrickson, 1977; Wong-Riley, 1977; 
Curcio and Harting, 1978; Lund et al., 1981; Baleydier and 
Mauguiere, 1985). Corticopulvinar connections have simi- 
larly been mapped by tracer experiments using WGA-HRP 
or similar labels. 
Fewer studies have been carried out at the level of 
microcircuitry. These mainly address corticopulvinar termi- 
nations. Golgi studies have described these terminations as 
classical type 1 (Guillery, 1971; Ogren and Hendrickson, 
1979a; Jones, 19851, that is, thin axons with small terminal 
specializations, in contrast to the larger (type 2) endings 
associated with specific sensory inputs. However, electron 
microscopy of corticopulvinar terminations in PI and PL, 
after isotope injections in areas V1 and V2 of the macaque 
(Ogren and Hendrickson, 1979a,b), has identified two 
populations. One is mainly small in size and located on 
distal dendrites. There is a secondary admixture (5%) of 
larger terminals, located more proximally. 
Two types of corticopulvinar terminations have been 
demonstrated recently by anterogradely transported PHA-L 
after cortical injections (Rockland, 1994). One type corre- 
sponds to small, stalked (type 1) terminations, and there is 
indeed a second, less frequent type that consists of large 
beaded terminations. The present investigation extended 
these findings by serial section reconstruction of longer 
segments of individual corticopulvinar axons. Serial section 
analysis allows investigation of quantitatively precise param- 
eters of arbor size and shape, divergence and convergence, 
and terminal size, shape, and number. Because of its high 
resolution, PHA-L can further reveal whether different 
types of terminations are intermixed or preferentially 
distributed within particular terminal foci or pulvinar 
subdivisions. The present study also addressed the possible 
generality of the corticopulvinar duality by comparisons 
with several other corticosubcortical connections. 
Another goal was to investigate the projections from 
ventromedial temporal areas as part of an extended compari- 
son of connectional patterns of early and higher order 
visual areas (Rockland and Van Hoesen, 1994). Are there, 
for example, features in the size or configuration of tempo- 
K.S. ROCKLAND 
common reference fbr previous studies. However, some cross-references are 
given to t.he recent terminology of Gutierrez et al. (19951, and the reader is 
referred to that paper for further discussion and comparisons with other 
current nomenclatures (Beneveuto and Davis, 1977; Bender, 1981; Unger- 
leider et al., 1983, 1984; Kms and Huerta, 1988; Robinson and Petersen,

ral corticopulvinar terminations that might be associated 
with properties, related to receptive field size or topo- 
graphic organization, of areas beyond V1 and V'? 
In brief, our results showed that the two types of 
corticopulvinar terminations are associated with very differ- 
ent arbor geometry. Large, beaded endings concentrate in 
sharply delimited, round or spherical arbors, but the smaller, 
spinous endings are densely arrayed along highly divergent 
and elongated, beam-like terminal portions. This duality is 
characteristic of corticopulvinar connections from areas V1 
and V2, as well as from temporal areas, but so far has not 
been observed in other corticosubcortical systems. 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
General procedures 
Seven macaque monkeys (3.5-5.0 kg; two M. mulatta and 
five M. fascicularis) received injections in occipitotemporal 
regions. Monkeys were deeply anesthetized with Nembutal 
(25 mg/kg) and prepared for sterile surgery, which was 
carried out according to approved protocols. Briefly, a 
craniotomy was performed to expose part of the superior 
temporal sulcus (STS) and middle temporal sulci, anterior 
and posterior (tmas and tmps). For injections in the lateral 
surface (cases 8 and 141, these sulcal landmarks were used 
to estimate the location of temporal areas TEO and TE 
(Bonin and Bailey, 1947). For more ventromedial place- 
ments, either an indirect approach was used from the 
lateral surface (cases 6 and 7), or, after gentle retraction 
and elevation of the cortex (following injection of mannitol 
to reduce cerebral volume), injections were made under 
direct visualization in relation to the occipitotemporal 
sulcus (OTS). Two or three injections of 2.5% PHA-L were 
made iontophoretically, except in one case (case 14) in 
which 10% biotinylated dextran amine (BDA) was injected 
instead. Animals were allowed to recover and then 12-18 
days later were reanesthetized and perfused transcardially 
with 4% paraformaldehyde, sometimes with 0.25% glutard- 
dehyde. Brains were serially sectioned at 50 pm on a 
freezing microtome and treated for PHA-L or BDA accord- 
ing to standard procedures (see Rockland and Virga, 1989 
for further technical details). Additional animals, used in 
previous studies, had injections of PHA-L in areas V1 or V2. 
The pulvinar was scanned for label in eight of these animals 
for comparison of termination types and patterns. One 
other animal had a BDA injection in the lateral pulvinar. In 
this case, because of technical factors probably related to 
postinjection survival interval (18 days), labeling was mainly 
retrograde, but this tissue was useful for verifying features 
of corticopulvinar projection neurons. Tissue from several 
normal brains, processed for cell bodies or myelin, was 
available for architectonic analysis. Sections from two of 
these brains had been treated for an antibody to neurofila- 
ment protein (SMI 32), which has been used in other 
studies to corroborate areal boundaries further (Hof and 
Morrison, 1995). 
Analysis 
Several subdivisions of inferotemporal cortex have been 
proposed in the macaque monkey (Seltzer and Pandya, 
1978; Baylis et al., 1987; Iwai and Yukie, 1987; Felleman 
and Van Essen, 1991; Martin-Elkins and Horel, 1992; 
Weller and Steele, 1992 (squirrel monkey); Suzuki and 
Amaral, 1994; Webster et al., 1994). In this study, we have 
assessed the identity of injected areas primarily by their
